---
title: "Marketing Analysis"
author: "Vibhor Dabas"
format:
  html: default
  pdf: default
editor: visual
---

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| message: false

library(tidyverse)
library(ggplot2)
library(knitr)
library(lubridate)


```

## INTRODUCTION

Many companies spend heavily on marketing their products, aiming to increase customer purchases. However, it is still a matter of doubt whether it really increases purchases when a company spends a large sum of money on marketing. This report analyses marketing spend and purchase data. The main objective is to analyse whether an increase in marketing spending results in improved purchase outcomes.

## RESEARCH QUESTION

The main idea behind this report is to determine whether increasing marketing spend per customer results in a larger purchase amount. Understanding this relationship can help businesses optimise their marketing strategies. Also, identifying trends between marketing_spend and customer purchasing behaviour is a main objective of this report.

## DATASET INTRODUCTION

The dataset contains information about marketing spend and purchase amounts. It also records how much was spent on customers and their corresponding purchase value. To summarise, understanding the data and the relationship can help optimise marketing strategies by focusing on and identifying patterns between marketing spend and purchase amount.

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| message: false
raw_data <- read_csv("data/36619647_Vibhor_Dabas.csv")

```

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| message: false
raw_data <- read_csv("data/36619647_Vibhor_Dabas.csv")

processed_data <- raw_data |>
  mutate(signup_date = parse_date_time(signup_date,
           orders = c("ymd", "dmy", "mdy", "B d, Y"))) |>
   
  
  select(-notes) |>
  drop_na(purchase_amount)

```

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| message: false

write_csv(processed_data, "data/processed_data.csv")
```

### DATASET DESCRIPTION

Processed dataset contains data of 132 customers and 6 variables namely - customer id, signup date, marketing spend, visits, purchase amount and segment. Customers are further divided into three segments Consumers, Enterprises and SMB which will further help to conclude where marketing spend is most effective.

The raw data was cleaned using 3 steps, firstly signup_date column had data in three formats which was then converted to one. Secondly, using select function notes column was removed becasue it was irrelevant for our analysis. Finally, rows with NA values were removed from purchase_amount column. These steps ensured that data is suitable for analysis.

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| message: false

write_csv(processed_data, "data/processed_data.csv")
```

The cleaned or processed dataset contains `r nrow(processed_data)` that depicts number of rows and `r ncol(processed_data)` , comprising numeric and categorical variables.




```{r}
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| label: tbl-summary
#| tbl-cap: Summary average of key variables by segment

processed_data |>
  group_by(segment) |>
  summarise(
    avg_spend = mean(marketing_spend), 
  avg_purchase = mean(purchase_amount), 
  avg_visits = mean(visits)) |>
kable()
```
#### TABLE SUMMARY

The table above @tbl-summary depicts average of -
1. Marketing spend
2. Purchase amount
3. Customer Visits
among all three segments. It also shows how customer behaviour in different segments chnages by marketing efforts.


```{r}
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| label: fig-spend-purchase
#| fig-cap: "Marketing Spend vs Purchase Amount"

ggplot(processed_data, aes(x = marketing_spend, y = purchase_amount )) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm")





```

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| label: fig-spend-groups
#| fig-cap: "Purchase amount across"

ggplot(processed_data, aes(x = cut(marketing_spend, 3), y = purchase_amount)) +
  geom_boxplot()

```

## RESULTS

@fig-spend-purchase represents relationship between Marketing Spend and Purchase Amount. There is a clear positive trend depiciting that customers receiving higher marketing tends to spend more on purchases. However some variability exists but overall trend is upwards shown by "blue" line.

@fig-spend-groups supports observations of figure 1 by grouping customers into 3 different level of marketing spend. It also clearly indicates that increased marketing spend leads to more customer spending.

In conclusion, these findings from above figures suggests that higher marketing spend is associated with higher purchase amounts, answering our research question positively.
